content: As the crowd grew and speeches became more inflammatory, the Gen-Z team was requested to brief their group to prevent the movement from becoming leaderless. Security forces provided escorts for the students. Since the protesters lacked a sound system, the police coordinated with Durga Party Palace to facilitate its arrangement. The CDO and SP requested local political leaders to ensure their cadres did not infiltrate the Gen-Z protest. Despite repeated warnings to remain peaceful, the mob began vandalizing fire engines and setting fire to offices. The Security Committee contacted protest representatives via phone and used miking in the area, urging them to leave the Sub-Metropolitan premises to minimize damage.

Publicity regarding the prohibitory and curfew orders (issued at 3:30 PM under the Local Administration Act, 1971) was carried out via media, social networks, and miking at the protest site. Orders were repeatedly given to use minimum force only to control those on the frontline of the arson and attacks to prevent further casualties. Security forces detained 13 individuals involved in the vandalism and took them to the Area Police Office, Itahari.

Following the arson at the municipal office, the crowd moved toward the Area Police Office at 5:00 PM to demand the release of the 13 detainees. A mob of approximately 5,000 launched a targeted attack on the police office. To protect the office, weapons, documents, and other detainees, a joint force of Nepal Police and APF used tear gas, blank rounds, and live aerial firing to disperse the crowd. The dispersing mob took some injured individuals with them, while the police rescued two injured persons and sent them to BPKIHS, Dharan, for treatment.

The use of firearms was not based on a specific written order but was a spontaneous act of self-defense following the principles of graduated force. Faced with a mob of 5,000 armed with stones, rods, and petrol bombs attempting to breach the detention center and potentially kill police personnel, the forces opened fire to prevent further loss of life and secure the premises.

The mob vandalized the digital display and vehicles parked at the police office. Several police officers were injured as protesters repeatedly attempted to force entry into the detention cells to free their colleagues. Arson attempts were made against various branches, communication equipment, and the weapons storage area within the police compound.

The Security Command Post decided to request reinforcements from the Bhagawati Dal Battalion (Nepal Army) in Itahari. An email request was sent at 4:03 PM, and the Army arrived at 5:24 PM. In the interim, the self-defense firing incident occurred during the attack on the police office. The Army team returned that night and was redeployed after 10:00 PM on Bhadra 24.

During the clashes, 10 Nepal Police personnel (including DSP Lakshmi Bhandari and several Inspectors) and 4 APF personnel were injured. Fifteen protesters were also reported injured. Evidence recovered from the scene included bricks, slingshots, and beer bottles smelling of petrol with cloth wicks (molotov cocktails). The mob utilized rods and bricks salvaged from demolished structures along the Asian Highway expansion project to carry out their attacks.

Analysis of the incident suggests causes included: a lack of leadership quality among Gen-Z leaders, infiltration by interest groups, and the viral spread of fake news on social media (e.g., false reports of bodies inside the Parliament building). Public frustration with the government and a shortage of security personnel relative to the crowd size also contributed. Furthermore, a lack of 'Unity of Command' was noted between the APF and the district command post. Participants included members of major political parties, religious extremists, and other interest groups. Efforts to identify those involved through social media footage are ongoing.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

Initially, 135 personnel (80 Police, 55 APF) were deployed at Golchowk. As numbers surged and the crowd turned violent, reinforcements were added, bringing the total to 363 personnel (222 Police under SP Sumun Kumar Timsina and 141 APF under SP Nirmal Kumar Thapa). After the arson at the municipal office at 3:20 PM, minimum force was used to push the mob out. When the mob attacked the police office at 4:50 PM, security forces used tear gas and aerial firing. Unfortunately, two protesters died during the clashes. On Bhadra 24, a massive jailbreak occurred at Jhumka Prison, where 1,796 inmates were forcibly freed by a mob of 500–600. Detailed damage reports for various government and private properties will be submitted. According to the then CDO Dharmendra Kumar Mishra, maximum restraint was exercised on Bhadra 24, resulting in no further serious injuries or deaths (Annex 6).
6.1.4. District Administration Office, Jhapa:

content.content: On Bhadra 22, youth groups requested permission for a peaceful rally. On Bhadra 23 at 10:35 AM, around 200 youths in Damak protested against the government's ban on social media, carrying banners against corruption and corrupt politicians.
Despite the organizers declaring the program over, the group marched to the Damak Municipality gate, vandalized CCTV cameras, and burned vehicles. Police used minor force to prevent further damage.

In Birtamode, a spontaneous gathering of 550 grew to 5,000. They attacked the Birtamode Municipality building, breaking windows and trapping the Deputy Mayor and staff inside. Security analysis suggested these incidents were exacerbated by internal political friction within both the Birtamode and Damak municipal leaderships.

Following the events in Kathmandu and the Home Minister's resignation, communication with the Ministry became difficult. To prevent night-time violence, curfews were imposed in Birtamode (starting 8:30 PM) and Damak (starting 10:00 PM) on Bhadra 23.

On Bhadra 24, the curfew was extended. However, crowds defied the order, gathering by 10:30 AM. In Birtamode, a large mob burned records and furniture at the Traffic Police Office and vandalized the Municipality building and various political party offices.

In Damak, the mob pelted stones and committed arson at the municipal office. Violence spread to surrounding municipalities (Gauradaha, Kankai, Arjundhara, etc.), where administrative buildings, ward offices, and customs offices were targeted.

By 2:00 PM, groups on motorcycles led attacks on the private homes of political representatives. In Damak, a mob brutally attacked the Area Police Office, looting weapons, ammunition, and uniforms. Similar looting occurred in Birtamode, where protesters even used the stolen weapons to fire at security forces. Gas cylinders were detonated at the Birtamode Traffic Police Office. Due to the massive crowd size, sending reinforcements or tear gas supplies was nearly impossible.

Three individuals died in Birtamode; it remains unclear if they were hit by police fire, fire from looted weapons, or injured during the accidental explosion of gas cylinders by the protesters themselves. Attacks on police units continued until 9:00 PM in rural areas.
Total munitions used in the district over Bhadra 23 and 24 included 496 aerial rounds and 254 tear gas shells by the Nepal Police, and 12 aerial rounds and 166 tear gas shells by the APF.

Causes of violence included local leadership disputes, the social media ban, and the inflammatory effect of photos showing casualties from Kathmandu. The participation of drug users, unemployed individuals, and those previously penalized by traffic police helped turn the crowd more aggressive. As police became occupied defending their own armories after 2:00 PM on the 24th, lawlessness increased.

content: Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

content.content: Starting from Bhadra 22, formal and informal meetings and discussions were held repeatedly among the members of the Security Committee.

From 11:00 AM on Bhadra 23, the heads of the Nepal Police, Armed Police Force (APF), and National Investigation Office remained in the Chief District Officer's (CDO) chamber to collect intelligence and provide necessary instructions to subordinates. Additional personnel were also deployed. On the morning of Bhadra 24, a command post involving Security Committee officials was established at the District Administration Office to monitor the situation and boost the morale of the forces. While Nepal Police and APF units were deployed throughout Bhadra 23 and 24, the Nepal Army joined for joint patrols after 11:00 PM on the 24th. Overall, coordination among security agencies was satisfactory, as stated in the incident report by the then CDO of Jhapa, Gopal Kumar Adhikari (Annex 6).

content: 6.2. Madhesh Province
6.2.1. District Administration Office, Parsa

On Bhadra 23, the Gen-Z generation organized an awareness program in Birgunj, the headquarters of Parsa, starting at 11:00 AM. They gathered at Ghantaghar for about an hour and conducted a peaceful rally demanding national interest, good governance, and corruption control. However, following news of deaths resulting from police force against young protesters in Kathmandu, the group reassembled. Around 5:00 PM, they gathered again at Ghantaghar to protest the Kathmandu fatalities. By 6:00-7:00 PM, the crowd became agitated, vandalizing traffic dividers and starting fires. When persuasion failed to control the crowd, a curfew was issued from 9:00 PM until 9:00 AM the next day (Bhadra 24) in specified areas of Birgunj to disperse the mob.

At 8:00 AM on Bhadra 24, an all-party meeting was held at the District Administration Office with political parties and security heads to assess potential damage and discuss whether to continue the curfew. Following this, a new curfew order was issued at 11:00 AM. While Nepal Police and APF were on the field, the Nepal Armyanticipating that their immediate involvement might lead to higher casualtiescoordinated with their high command while awaiting specific orders. Meanwhile, crowds in Birgunj grew as various groups merged, primarily gathering for slogans in front of Ghantaghar.

Between 1:00 PM and 2:00 PM, social media reports and videos showed damage to key structures in Kathmandu (Singha Durbar, International Convention Center, Supreme Court, President's Office) and the private residences of political leaders. This incited the local crowd to attack a police beat near Ghantaghar, the Nepal Telecom office, and vehicles. Subsequently, vandalism, looting, and arson spread to the Birgunj Metropolitan City office and private homes. After 10:00 PM that night, following a statement and order from the Chief of Army Staff, the Nepal Army was deployed. Coordinated efforts by all three security branches prevented further damage after 10:00 PM.

On Bhadra 23, some children escaped from the Juvenile Detention Center after breaking the gate, and an attempt to break the main prison gate was thwarted. On the morning of the 25th, after protesters tried to dig a hole in the prison compound wall to free inmates, security forces used miking to warn them and eventually used tear gas and aerial firing to prevent a jailbreak. Following these incidents, instances of looting and arson decreased, and the security situation gradually stabilized. From Bhadra 23 until the end of the month, the situation was normalized through coordinated patrolling and the declaration of curfews and riot-affected zones.

The attackers targeted almost all office buildings and vehicles of the Birgunj Metropolitan City. Significant arson and vandalism also occurred at the private residences of several leaders: Pradeep Yadav (former Minister/MP), Ajay Chaurasia (former Minister/MP), Raj Kumar Gupta (former Minister/MP), and Lal Babu Raut (former Chief Minister, Madhesh Province), where two four-wheelers were burned. Three out of four houses within the compound of Anil Rungta (Constituent Assembly member) were completely destroyed, and his vehicles were vandalized. During these fires, a worker named Shila Devi Dusarin was severely burned and later died during treatment in Kathmandu. One of the two houses belonging to Rajeshman Singh, Mayor of Birgunj, was completely destroyed, while the other suffered arson on the ground and first floors and vandalism on the upper floor.

Other damages included the Nepal Telecom office at Ghantaghar, where the ground floor and upper-floor batteries were destroyed, and seven vehicles (including cars, auto-rickshaws, and motorcycles) were burned. At the Inland Revenue Office, motorcycles were burned, and ground-floor records were destroyed by fire. Vandalism occurred at the High Court (Janakpur-Birgunj Bench), the Transport Management Office, and several ward offices of Birgunj Metropolitan City (9, 10, 11, 14, 21, 26, and 31). Furthermore, buildings belonging to Area Police, police posts, and beats under the District Police Office were targeted, the Parsa prison gate was damaged, and a jailbreak occurred at the Juvenile Detention Center.

The causes of the escalation were linked to the news of Gen-Z deaths in Kathmandu on Bhadra 23. This was exacerbated on Bhadra 24 by social media broadcasts showing the burning of major government buildings and the private residences of President Ram Chandra Paudel and leaders like K.P. Sharma Oli, Sher Bahadur Deuba, and Pushpa Kamal Dahal 'Prachanda'. This created a retaliatory mindset among the public against the leadership.

While the movement was initially attributed to Gen-Z youth, it is estimated that the vandalism, looting, and arson on Bhadra 24 involved infiltrators from other age groups, including unruly individuals, drivers, laborers, and factory workers.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:

There was a sound relationship among the Nepal Army, Nepal Police, APF, and National Investigation District Office throughout the events. A curfew was issued in anticipation of violence on Bhadra 24. Since maintaining peace with only the Police and APF proved nearly impossible, written and oral requests were made for Army deployment. However, per central command orders, the Army only moved into the field after 10:00 PM on the 24th. It is estimated that if joint deployment had occurred at 11:00 AM alongside the curfew, the day's destruction could have been prevented.

Since the Army's deployment at 10:00 PM on Bhadra 24, no significant incidents occurred except for minor vandalism at remote local ward offices. The then CDO of Parsa, Toyanarayan Subedi, submitted this statement (Annex 6) based purely on memory, as he is no longer in that office and could not consult current staff or records.

District Administration Office, Dhanusha: Per the Local Administration Act, 1971, the District Security Committee is chaired by the CDO and includes heads of the Army, Police, APF, and National Investigation Office, with the Assistant CDO as member-secretary. The committee analyzes security, collects intelligence, and decides on deployment plans, which were followed accordingly.
On Bhadra 23 and 24, security heads analyzed the situation in the CDO’s chamber. No force was used, and as a result, no individuals were injured in Dhanusha district.

However, private properties were attacked and houses were burned by various groups. Detailed records of these incidents are maintained at the District Police Office and can be provided to the Secretariat upon request.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies

The mutual relationship and coordination among the Chairman and members of the District Security Committee were exemplary and highly supportive. As a result, although there was damage to private and public property, no human casualties occurred. This is detailed in the incident report by the then Chief District Officer of Dhanusha, Shankarhari Acharya, in Annex 6.
6.2.3. District Administration Office, Rautahat:
The protests were driven by nationwide unrest and anger over the deaths and injuries during the Kathmandu movement on the 23rd, as well as general frustration regarding governance.

Protesters set fire to and damaged municipal buildings in Chandrapur, Phatuwa Vijaypur, Katahariya, and Garuda, which are located far from the district headquarters. Similarly, Maulapur, Bodhimai, Gaur, and Paroha municipalities were set on fire, causing partial damage to office buildings, while some vehicles in Maulapur, Chandrapur, and Garuda were burned. Regarding government offices, the Area Police Office in Chandrapur, Road Division Office, District Traffic Police Office, District Forest Division Office, and the Provincial Treasury and Accounts Controller Office were set on fire, resulting in damage to both buildings and vehicles. Minor damage occurred at some ward offices in Brindaban, Gadhimai, and Madhav Narayan municipalities. In terms of private property, the houses of Assistant CDO Kiran Nidhi Tiwari and former PM Madhav Kumar Nepal were damaged, and the house of Garuda Mayor Kanthamani was looted. On the 23rd, hundreds of protesters attempted arson and vandalism at the District Administration Office in Gaur and the District Court; however, the deployment of Armed Police Force (APF) No. 11 Battalion HQ and Nepal Police prevented major damage, resulting only in minor broken glass on buildings and vehicles.
No significant damage occurred in the district after the deployment of the Army on the night of the 24th.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies

The deployment of security agencies was excellent. Security personnel (APF and Nepal Police) were extensively deployed in a coordinated manner to maintain public safety and peace. Except for minor incidents, no major damage occurred in the headquarters, Gaur. However, despite the efforts of security forces, protesters managed to cause damage in municipalities far from the headquarters.

On the 24th, a security command post was established at the District Administration Office building to coordinate peace and security arrangements. The CDO remained at the office, establishing the command post and deploying APF and Nepal Police. Between 1:30 PM and 4:45 PM, protesters repeatedly tried to enter the District Administration Office premises to cause damage, but through the use of minimum force, no damage occurred in the headquarters. Around 10:30 PM on the 24th, some inmates escaped from the District Jail but were immediately brought under control by security forces. However, about 131 inmates (out of 291) remained at large, according to the incident report by the then CDO of Rautahat, Binod Kumar Khadka, in Annex 6.
6.3. Bagmati Province
6.3.1. District Administration Office, Kavrepalanchok:

Before the events started in Kathmandu on August 23, 2082, the district situation was normal. The District Security Committee was focused on ensuring safe travel on the B.P. Highway (damaged by previous rains) and preparing for the upcoming Dashain and Tihar festivals, including road upgrades and disaster management. Upon receiving news of the unpleasant events and curfew in Kathmandu at 1:00 PM, an emergency Security Committee meeting was called. That evening, a discussion was held regarding a request for a peaceful program in Banepa Bazaar by Ayush Tiwari on behalf of Gen-Z youth. It was decided to deploy Nepal Police and APF to Banepa and other sensitive areas. Special vigilance was maintained to prevent outside infiltration into the Gen-Z crowd. Around 50 Gen-Z youths began a peaceful program with placards, but when 200–300 others tried to join, security separated the groups. Due to provocative speeches by the infiltrating group, additional security was added, and the program concluded peacefully around 8:30 PM.

On the morning of August 24, 2082, the security command post became active. The committee directed high-alert deployment and instructed the Nepal Army’s Shri Mehar Battalion in Dhulikhel to remain on standby. Essential services were also alerted. By 9:30 AM, reports of planned protests and potential violence in Banepa and Panauti emerged, leading to an immediate analysis by the security committee.

As crowds began gathering in an unorganized manner, and considering the curfew in the Kathmandu Valley, it was decided to issue a curfew order for Banepa, Panauti, Dhulikhel, and Panchkhal starting at 12:15 PM under the Local Administration Act, 1971. The Shri Mehar Battalion was requested for deployment and immediately mobilized. The curfew was publicized via media and social networks, and joint security teams used miking to inform the public. Despite limited equipment, security forces made maximum efforts to restrain a violent crowd of 8,000–10,000 using minimum force. To protect government and private property, tear gas, lathi charges, and aerial firing were used as per the law and Ministry of Home Affairs instructions.

Around 1:00 PM, protests and rallies escalated into violent activities in Banepa and Panauti, surpassing the capacity of available security personnel. Security chiefs were instructed through the command post to maintain order within the legal framework of the Local Administration Act using minimum force. These instructions specifically emphasized human rights, the district's situation, and the previous day's incidents in Kathmandu to ensure a balanced response. Coordinated action among all security units was mandated.

As reports of the uncontrolled and destructive mob in Banepa and Panauti came in, the command post provided continuous direction. The chaotic crowd set fire to and vandalized the Banepa Municipal Office, Area Police Office Banepa, Nepali Congress and CPN-UML party offices, the District Traffic Police Office, and various police posts. Arson and vandalism also targeted the private homes of the Mayor and Deputy Mayor of Banepa. Similarly, the Panauti Municipal Office, various ward offices, and police posts in Panauti, Panchkhal, and Hinguwapati were attacked. Protesters also targeted the houses and vehicles of the Mayor of Dhulikhel, the Mayor, Deputy Mayor, and former Mayor of Panauti, Constituent Assembly member Shiva Humagai, and social worker Rameshwar Thapa. Detailed damage reports will be provided by the District Administration and Police offices.

On August 25, an attempt to instigate arson and a jailbreak at Dhulikhel Prison, led by an inmate named Sagar Mainali (alias Ram Krishna Mainali), was thwarted by deploying additional security and using minimal force. While minor fire damage occurred, the prison and inmates remained secure. Four individuals involved in the jailbreak attempt were transferred to the District Police custody and subsequently to the Central Jail in Kathmandu.

Security forces successfully protected the district headquarters and government offices by blocking the mob at Baghchok, Dhulikhel, through strategic deployment and minimum force. The restraint, patience, and courage of the personnel were exemplary. After five hours of clashes, the situation was brought under control, and the Nepal Army took over security at 10:00 PM. The curfew was extended and eventually lifted on the night of August 28.

Overall, in Kavre district, no one was killed or significantly injured during the movement. No security weapons were looted, though reports from the Area Police Office in Banepa indicated some missing ammunition.
Deployment and Coordination of Security Agencies:
